text.fullText: Perez, JuanB From: Chapman, Eric, ENV <eric.chapman@env.nm.gov> Sent: Wednesday, January 21, 2026 6:46 PM To: OW-Docket; cwa401 Cc: Keyes, Christina, ENV; jonas.armstrong2@env.nm.gov; shelly.lemon@env.nm.gov Subject: Request for extension for Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 Some people who received this message don't often get email from eric.chapman@env.nm.gov. Learn why this is important Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. Good afternoon, The New Mexico Environment Department (NMED) respectfully requests a 30-day extension of the public comment period for the proposed rule, "Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations," published in the Federal Register on January 15, 2026. The current 30-day comment period, scheduled to close on February 17, 2026, does not provide sufficient time for a meaningful and thorough technical review of a rulemaking that fundamentally alters the landscape of Clean Water Act (CWA) implementation. As the state agency responsible for protecting New Mexico's water resources, NMED requires additional time to provide the EPA with the robust, data-driven feedback necessary for such a significant regulatory shift for the following reasons:  Complexity of Technical and Legal Revisions: This proposal represents a near-total reversal of the 2023 "Water Quality Certification Improvement Rule." Evaluating the impacts of shifting from an "activity as a whole" standard to a "discharge-only" standard requires coordination across multiple technical bureaus within NMED, including our Surface Water Quality, Ground Water Quality, and Legal divisions.  Unique Regional Impacts: New Mexico faces unique hydrological challenges, including a high percentage of ephemeral and intermittent waters. We must conduct a detailed assessment of how the narrowed "scope of review" will interact with recent changes to the definition of "Waters of the United States" (WOTUS). A 30-day window is inadequate to model the potential loss of state oversight for critical projects in our arid environment.  Inter-Agency and Tribal Coordination: NMED works closely with 23 sovereign tribes, nations, and pueblos within New Mexico. Many of these tribal partners are also "certifying authorities" under Section 401. A 30-day extension is necessary to facilitate meaningful consultation and to ensure that the state’s comments reflect a comprehensive understanding of the shared impacts on our water basins.  Public Participation: Given the high level of public interest in water security in the Southwest, NMED intends to gather input from local stakeholders and agricultural communities. The current deadline severely limits our ability to engage these constituents and incorporate their concerns into our official filing. 1 In light of the significant consequences this rule holds for state sovereignty and public health, we believe a 30-day extension is both warranted and necessary to uphold the CWA’s statutory obligation to provide a "reasonable period for participation." We thank you for your consideration of this request and look forward to your response. Respectfully, Eric Chapman, Ph.D. Science Coordinator - Office of Strategic Initiatives New Mexico Environment Department Harold Runnels Building 1190 S St. Francis Dr Santa Fe, NM 87505 Eric.Chapman@env.nm.gov (505) 629-3551 2
